title: Chapter 21: The Distance
content: I give him space.

That's what you're supposed to do when someone asks for it. You breathe. You step back. You don't crowd them with your need or your fear or the four hundred years of pattern you carry like a scar that won't stop throbbing. You just — give them space.

Rowan said he needed time. His voice in the archive was quiet, bewildered, like a man who'd opened a door in his own house and found a room he didn't know existed. Not cruel. Never cruel. That's what made it worse. Cruelty I can metabolize. Cruelty has edges I can grip. But bewilderment is formless, and I've learned that formless things are the ones that swallow you.

William said the same thing. Different words, same architecture: *I need to understand this. Give me a moment.* That was 1867. He was standing in the parlor of our rented rooms on the Via de' Tornabuoni, and the morning light was doing something cruel to his face — making it honest. I watched him rub his jaw the way he did when he was working through a problem in his architecture, and I understood that I had become a problem. Something to solve. Something with a solution that probably excluded me.
He called me an abomination three days later.
I don't think Rowan will use that word. He doesn't have it in him. But I've been wrong before about which words people have in them. The ones you think are missing are only sleeping.
So I wait.

The studio swallows me. I let it. The dress form in the corner wears a half-finished gown — the Oakes commission, midnight silk with the obsidian beadwork I was so excited about two weeks ago. I look at it now and feel nothing. The beads are still beautiful. The silk still falls the way I shaped it to fall. But the excitement has drained out of me like heat from a room where someone's left the window open.

There's a crack in the studio window. I noticed it last week, a thin vein running from the lower corner, and I keep meaning to call the building manager, but I haven't. The night air comes through it — cold, metallic, laced with the distant sweetness of the rotting leaves piled along the curb four stories below. The smell of a city surrendering to November. It should be unpleasant. Instead it's the only thing that feels real.

I work. Sort of. My hands move through the motions of pinning and cutting and adjusting, but it's like watching someone else's hands from very far away. The fabric whispers against the shears. The needle catches light. I am building a dress for a woman I've never met for a party I'll never attend, and the normalcy of that is almost funny.
Almost.
The hum has gone quiet.

Not silent — never silent — but grief-quiet. That specific attenuation I've learned to recognize over centuries, the way you recognize the sound of your own breathing when you're sick: louder in the silence, heavier, wrong. When the hum is fueled by love, it resonates. It fills the spaces between my ribs with warmth. It makes me feel infinite. But grief-quiet is different. It's the hum pulling inward, coiling, condensing. Building pressure.

That's how shifts work. Not always the way people imagine — not always some dramatic, cinematic metamorphosis triggered by passion or fury. Sometimes the shift builds like a storm behind your sternum, fed by the slow, steady accumulation of loss. Every hour Rowan doesn't call is another drop of barometric pressure. Every professional text is another thread of cold front moving in.
The worst sound in the world isn't the hum at full roar. It's the hum going quiet. Because quiet means it's storing. And stored shifts don't emerge gently.
A week.
Seven days measured in texts that arrive like telegrams from a country that used to be ours.
*The fabric samples have arrived.*

Five words. I read them fifteen times. I count. Not deliberately — my eyes just keep returning, scanning for something between the letters. A warmth. A question. A *how are you* or *I miss you* or even just his name for me — any version of it. But there's nothing between those letters except the professional distance of colleagues who have never touched each other in the dark.
*The atelier schedule is updated.*

I reply in kind. Brief. Clean. No residue. *Received, thank you.* *Noted.* Each response costs me something I can't name, some small coin of self I surrender to maintain the fiction that I'm fine, that I'm giving him the space he asked for, that this is normal and temporary and not the beginning of the ending I've lived through before.
I don't tell him I've started sleeping at the studio.

There's a couch in the back room — Victorian, horsehair, meant for clients waiting for fittings. It's not comfortable. The cushion has a crater in the center that my body has started shaping itself around, and there's a spring that catches my hip if I turn the wrong way. But going home means walking past his building, and I can't do that yet. I can't pass the brass awning and the doorman who knows us both and the lobby light that's always on, warm and steady, promising a kind of belonging I no longer trust.

So I sleep here. I wake with the taste of nothing in my mouth — not bad, not good, just the absent flavor of a body that hasn't eaten since yesterday. My stomach is a hollow thing, echoing. I should eat. I know this. The shift feeds on depletion, and every skipped meal is fuel for the thing building behind my ribs. But eating requires appetite, and appetite requires desire, and desire is the problem, isn't it? Desire is what got me here. Desire is what always gets me here.

The studio smells like starch and cold coffee and the mineral tang of pins scattered on the cutting table. I breathe it in and think: this is what a life looks like when you subtract a person from it. Smaller. More mineral. Less human.
Suki tells me.

She doesn't mean to — or maybe she does. Suki has never believed in sparing people. She believes information is neutral and the receiver's reaction is their own business, which is a philosophy that works beautifully for a gallery owner and terribly for a friend.
"There was a fundraiser," she says over the phone, her voice carefully uninflected. "The Met board thing. Rowan was there."
I wait. I know there's more. Suki pauses in that specific way that means she's about to hand me something I won't want to hold.
"He was with someone. A woman."

I don't ask for details. I don't need them. I can see her already — not her specific face but her type. The sort of woman who belongs at a Met board fundraiser beside a man like Rowan. Polished. Brought up in the right houses and the right schools. A person whose body has never shifted, whose identity has never rewritten itself, whose existence comes with a fixed category that the world knows how to file.
Appropriate.

That's the word that lands in me like a pin in fabric, sharp and precise. Appropriate. He is trying to prove to himself that he's still the person he was before he met me. The person whose attraction had a fixed category. The person who was straight. Not questioning, not expanding, not standing in the doorway of that unknown room he discovered in his own house wondering what it means that the room exists at all.

He's going back to the blueprint. I understand. I do. When the architecture of yourself starts to shift, the first instinct is to reach for the original drawing. To prove the foundation is still sound. To stand in the house you know even if the walls are shaking.
I understand. Understanding doesn't stop it from hurting.
Evening. The studio window again. I press my forehead against the glass and let the cold have me.

The glass is smoother than skin, colder than intention. It holds the October chill the way my chest holds the quiet hum — faithfully, without complaint. Below, the city blurs. Lights bleeding into each other, taillights and streetlamps and the amber glow of apartments where people are living their single-category lives without knowing how lucky they are. My breath fogs the glass. I watch it bloom and fade. Bloom and fade.
I think about William.

Not the William who called me an abomination — I've worn that memory smooth as river stone, it barely cuts anymore. I think about the William before. The one who loved me with his whole body, who held my face in both hands like it was something precious, who told me I was the most beautiful creature God had ever thought to make. He meant it. I believe he meant it. That's the part people don't understand — they hear *abomination* and assume the love before it was a lie, but it wasn't. It was real. It was simply conditional on a version of me that couldn't last.
Because I shift.

That's the thing about loving someone like me: the body you love is a temporary address. The face you memorize will change. The planes and curves that wire your desire will rearrange, and you'll be left holding attraction to a person who no longer matches the shape of it. William couldn't hold both — his love for me and his love for the woman I was. When the woman changed, the love curdled. Not into indifference. Into something worse. Horror. The horror of discovering your desire has a boundary you didn't know about, and the person you loved has crossed it without asking, and now you're standing on the wrong side of your own want.
Maybe this is the pattern.

The thought arrives the way thoughts do when the hum goes grief-quiet — not sudden but inevitable, like water finding the crack. Maybe love is the trigger. Not the cause but the catalyst. I love, and the love provokes the shift, and the shift destroys the love, and then I grieve, and the grief provokes the next shift, and the next shift prepares the ground for the next love, because desire doesn't learn from history. Desire is the stupidest part of immortality. It keeps reaching for the flame no matter how many times the palm has been burned.

Four hundred years. I have had this body less than two of them. I have been a man for centuries at a stretch. I have been loved as a man and abandoned as a man and it never hurt less than this, and it never hurt more than this, because Rowan is not William and that's the cruelest part — each one is different enough to make me believe this time the pattern won't hold.
The hum is so quiet.

I press harder against the glass. The cold bites. I let it. My stomach is a pit, empty and sour, and I can't remember the last time I ate anything with intention. The studio crack lets in a thread of November — rotting leaves and exhaust and the faint chemical sweetness of the textile district — and I breathe it like it's medicine.

That's how I know the grief is building toward something catastrophic. Not because of the size of the feeling but because of the quality of the silence. The hum doesn't go quiet when it's done. It goes quiet when it's beginning. The pause before the orchestra re-enters. The held breath before the plunge.
I've been here before. I know what comes next. And I still can't stop it.
My phone buzzes.
The vibration travels through the cutting table and into my palms where they rest on the wood — a small violence, a tiny earthquake. I look down. The screen is lit.
Rowan.
Two words.
*I'm sorry.*

I stare at it. The letters are just pixels. Arranged light. They don't mean anything until I read them, and reading is a choice, and I'm not sure I want to make it. But I've already read them. That's the trick with words — you can't unread them. They're in me now, settling into the same place where William's *abomination* still lives, where every quiet withdrawal has taken up residence over four centuries of believing and losing and believing again.
*I'm sorry.*

I know what it means. I've known what it means since he asked for time. It means he's sorry he can't be what I need. It means he's choosing the person he was over the person I showed him he could be. It means the room he found in his house scared him, and he's bricked up the door, and he's sorry about that — genuinely sorry — but sorry doesn't change the wall.
I pick up the phone. My thumb hovers over the keyboard.
I type: *Don't be.*
Then I delete it. That's too kind. Too forgiving. Too much like the woman who keeps making excuses for people who leave.
I type: *I understand.*
Then I delete it. That's too true. Too resigned. Too much like someone who saw this coming and still couldn't get out of the way.
I type nothing.

I set the phone face down on the cutting table. The screen light leaks around the edges for a moment — a thin halo — and then it goes dark. The studio returns to its own dimness, the dress form white as bone in the corner, the pins catching nothing.
The hum is a whisper now.

Not grief-quiet anymore. Something else. Something lower, deeper, gathering in the spaces between my ribs where love used to live. The silence before the shift. The held breath of four hundred years of the same story, the same withdrawal, the same brick wall, the same sorry.

I close my eyes. The city hums outside — ambulances and taxis and the lives of people who don't know what's building in this room. The cold glass waits for my forehead. The crack in the window breathes. My empty stomach folds in on itself. And somewhere beneath my skin, beneath the silence, beneath the sorry I won't answer, the shift is learning my grief by heart.

This is the pattern. I know it like I know my own hands — because they've been three hundred different pairs of hands, and every single one of them has held a phone it couldn't answer, has pressed itself against a window it couldn't open, has waited for a word that never came.
The pattern is repeating.
And I'm too heartbroken to break it.
